content.full_text:
FPPC

AUG 0 9 19 AM '90
John H. Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, # 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

August 2, 1990

Re: Request for Opinion Concerning Potential Conflict

Dear Mr. Larson:

I am writing this letter to obtain guidance on a possibly
sticky issue. I am an attorney and Councilmember in the City of
Alameda as well as the Republican nominee for the 13th Assembly
District.

In January 1990, I became associated with a law firm, MEYER
& MITCHELL (hereinafter M&M), that up until November 1989, had
worked on a contract basis for the City of Alameda. (M&M's
representation of the City was terminated at the November 7,
1989, Council meeting effective immediately although the City
Attorney and Councilmember Withrow somehow transformed immediate
termination into meaning M&M should complete any motions which
were then outstanding, there being one motion pending in federal
court which I believe was heard in early December 1989.)

I pick my own cases and am not an employee of the firm. I
receive compensation based on the work performed with no specific
requirement that I work a set number of hours or cases. Indeed,
with all of the positions I hold on both City and County Boards,
my Assembly campaign, and being a wife and mother of two
children, there are many demands on my time which take a much
higher priority than working as a paid attorney for others.

Prior to my association with M&M, I sought and obtained
legal advice from both my City Attorney, Carol Korade, and the
FPPC (oral), that there was no conflict. I was simply advised to
abstain on any matters concerning M&M if they came up. I believe
there was a final payment of fees that was approved on the
consent calendar and from which I abstained prior to going to
work and also prior to discussing any financial terms of
association with Mr. Mitchell.

The issue of a potential conflict has arisen in that the
firm represents several police officers in a malicious
prosecution case. M&M had been retained by the City to defend
the City against the underlying lawsuit. The police officers
retained M&M prior to M&M not for defense but for affirmative
relief. After the City terminated M&M, the police officers
continued M&M's representation as to the malicious prosecution.
Prior to my association with M&M and in the exercise of my
responsibilities as a Councilmember, I had been contacted by the
police officers with their concerns about the quality of
representation by the then City Attorney, Carter Stroud. I
reviewed his work on the case, and had some concerns myself.
Thereafter, Mr. Stroud resigned employment with the City of
Alameda. It has now become apparent to me that Mr. Stroud's
actions may have seriously jeopardized the rights of the officers
to recover in the context of a malicious prosecution suit, i.e.
he did not have express authority to settle the case on their

behalf and settled the case in spite of that lack of authority on
behalf of all parties.
The malicious prosecution suit at this point has received a
defense summary judgment and is on appeal to the First Appellate
District Civil No. A049265 by the officers. The facts supporting
my conclusions are fully supported in the Appellants' Opening
Brief. M&M is handling the appeal, though I am not involved. If
the officers lose the appeal I am concerned that they will have a
right to proceed against the City of Alameda and the City
Attorney, Carter Stroud, and must be so advised. The better action
is for them to be so advised at this point in time so that
they can address the malpractice issue as soon as possible.

While I am not playing any role in this case, I am very
concerned as a Councilmember that the City Attorney's office is
continuing with this quality of representation of our employees.
One of the three police officers has brought another malicious
prosecution action in another case; again using M&M. Prior to
the officer bringing the malicious prosecution action and in my
role as Councilmember, I became aware that the City was being
asked to settle the underlying case. I did not participate in
any Council vote concerning the case, and don't believe there was
one. [The majority of the City Council has by formal action
given the new City Attorney, Carol Korade, the right to settle
cases up to $25,000 and to hire and fire outside Counsel in what
appears to me to be a direct conflict with the specific
requirements of our Charter and clearly an abuse of discretion by
the Council.]

During the defense of the underlying case, the City
Attorney and outside Counsel [BERTRAND, FOX, ELLIOT & COHN],
appeared in federal court, the Honorable Judge Vukasin presiding,
and settled the case on behalf of the City, again over the
objections of the officer who was present in Court. They filed
a document which clearly indicated that they were simply
overruling the officer's objections and settling the case; they
made clear that there was a conflict in their representation of
the officer and the City pointed out to both the Court and
opposing counsel that the officer didn't want to settle because
he wanted to bring a malicious prosecution suit against the
plaintiff. The officer was not consulted before the City
Attorney so informed the Court. Indeed he had asked the City
Attorney and outside counsel for a copy of the document that they
had filed with the Court; they both declined to show it to the
officer or give him a copy. As an attorney I am aware of such
things as the attorney client privilege, conflicts when
representing multiple parties and the applicable ethics statutes
and guidelines. Clearly, Carol Korade did not have written
authorization to represent the officer, he had never requested it
under Govt. Code 825a nor did she or outside counsel have his
signed consent to settle the case which is required under the
State Bar rules. After disclosing confidential statements to
Carol Korade and BERTRAND FOX, ELLIOT & COHN, they apparently
violated his rights to confidentiality which I believe applies in
this case even if they did not actually represent the officer.

Again, I am faced with the dilemma that I am aware of the

quality of legal representation coming out of the City of Alameda
and that the firm I am associated with, has a responsibility to
advise their clients, in this case the police officer, that he
has been the victim of malpractice by the City Attorney and
outside counsel she has hired without City Council approval.

I have tried my best to balance my responsibilities
without infringing upon the City Attorney's jurisdiction.
However, I cannot let the quality of representation continue for
the City's employees. Yet there is nothing the majority of the
Council will do about it. I have so notified the Council
repeatedly. My last resort has been to advise the City Manager
of the quality of representation and that in my opinion, the City
Attorney is generating new causes of action for malpractice
against the City of Alameda with her continuing quality of
representation. His response is that there is nothing that he
can do about it without Council action. I advised him that if
he can do nothing to stop it and to see that his employees get
competent representation, that he can anticipate the financial
impact upon the City's financial resources and the long term
impact on his employees morale.

(*NOTE: In the last month, the Assistant City Manager sought
legal advice from the City Attorney concerning the requirements
of giving notice for a Special Meeting of an advisory board; she
advised him that none was required. He then participated in a
meeting with the majority of that Board's members and discussed
the subjects which that board regulates, Cable TV.
Unfortunately, the City Attorney did not advise the Assistant
City Manager that the meeting had to open and public and the City
has now had to cure the Brown Act violation as best as possible
retroactively. This is not the least nor the most significant
shortfall in legal representation that this City is suffering, it
is simply one of the most recent.)

I have been in contact with the Grand Jury repeatedly
concerning Brown Act violations that have occurred both upon Ms.
Korade's advice and with her presence. I am not seeking
confirmation of these violations, I already have that from the
Grand Jury. I am seeking an opinion as to what my role should be
to cure them in the future given the constraint that the majority
of the council continues to unquestioningly follow her advice.

The City Attorney insisted upon the propriety of a closed
session to discuss and agendize confidential
employee pay resolution of which she is a member as are the City
Manager, Asst. City Manager, Personnel Director, all of whom
were present at the majority of the meeting. She attended
five of six closed meetings where fringe benefits and a 5% pay
raise were discussed for the group. I told the City Manager that
I felt very uncomfortable discussing these items with the four
members of the Management wage group present, and he told me that
at the next meeting he would ask if anyone wanted him to leave.
He did so, and no one spoke up in the session (I had already
objected) so he and everyone else remained. They discussed the
5% pay raise and a demand by the Police management for a 9.8% pay
raise. Prior to the next meeting, I again presented my concerns
to the City Manager, he countered with his actions at the last

meeting. I told him that irrespective of what any other
councilmember stated or did not state, I would not honor them
if we went into closed session to discuss these items again, if
he and the management group remained. At the next meeting, he
and the City Attorney left, however, the Asst. City Manager and
Personnel director remained. I remained at the meeting but did
not actively participate. Nor did the Mayor, who simply asked
for more information concerning salaries.

At the last special meeting scheduled for consideration of
the pay raise, the Mayor did not take us into closed session as
the press was beginning to have a field day with what was
transpiring behind closed doors. The City Attorney indicated
that now we could not discuss salaries, fringes, etc. all those
things which we had already discussed and on some reached a
consensus. My concern is what do we do now to cure the
violations that have already occurred? We are going to meet
within a few days, and I am concerned that what we should do is
start from scratch. I am not sure exactly what that means or how
to implement it at this time. I am seeking an opinion as to what
we should do to cure these violations and to avoid their
reoccurrence.

I am also specifically seeking an opinion as to how I
should deal with the present situation with the City Attorney.
She has apparently advised the other councilmembers that I could
resign either my position as a councilmember, or terminate my
association with M&M to eliminate any conflicts. Indeed, she has
offered as evidence to the other councilmembers that upon seeing
a police office outside her door, I told him I hoped he wasn't
being represented by her office. My complete statement to him,
which I jokingly offered, was that I had hoped he hadn't done
anything for which he needed to be represented by her office; I
see it as a very serious situation anytime the City is sued and
especially to the police officer when he or she is sued. I
didn't know the officer and had no reason to believe he had done
anything to need legal representation.

When asked about my apparent options as they now stand, the
City Attorney has refused to render a legal opinion to me. While
I realize that she personally has a conflict in so doing, she
does not render legal opinions personally, but rather hires
attorneys to provide legal services for her. I see no reason why
she cannot hire independent counsel to advise me as to whether or
not I have a conflict in performing my official responsibilities
and how to best alleviate any such conflicts, especially since
she has hired outside counsel to perform the inquiry for
Councilmember Withrow.

I am forwarding you a few pertinent pieces of documentation
that you may get a full grasp of the situation. If I may be
of any assistance please don't hesitate to call.

Yours very truly,

Barbara M. Thomas
Barbara M. Thomas

sections.question_synthetic: An attorney serving as both a city councilmember and Assembly candidate seeks FPPC guidance on potential conflicts of interest arising from her association with a law firm that previously represented the city and now represents police officers in litigation against the city, as well as guidance on her role in addressing Brown Act violations and conflicts involving the City Attorney.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal question or conclusion sections are present. The document is heavily narrative in nature with multiple complex fact patterns and issues interwoven. The letter appears incomplete (truncated). High complexity and length make full extraction challenging. No clear delineation of specific legal questions asked.

embedding.summary: A city councilmember and attorney requests FPPC guidance on multiple conflict of interest issues including her association with a law firm representing police officers in litigation against the city, her concerns about the City Attorney's legal representation quality and alleged Brown Act violations, and her role in addressing these governance issues.
